mnemonics: Metric Spaces = Measurable, Everywhere, Triangles Rule (remember triangle inequality)
keyPoints: A metric space is a set with a distance function (metric) that satisfies non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.
The concept generalizes the notion of distance from Euclidean space to more abstract sets.
Key examples include Euclidean spaces, discrete metric spaces, and function spaces with the supremum norm.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to verify all four metric properties (especially the triangle inequality) when proving a function is a metric.
Confusing metric spaces with topological spaces (a metric induces a topology, but not all topologies come from a metric).
Assuming all familiar rules from Euclidean space (like inner products) apply to all metric spaces.
